message.abstract:
<jats:title>Abstract</jats:title>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Introduction</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>the COVID-19 pandemic amplified the need for robust multi-sectoral coordination; yet the specific mechanisms, benefits, and challenges of such collaboration particularly in low-and middle-income countries (LMICs) remain poorly synthesised.</jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Objective</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>To identify the key elements, benefits, challenges, and improvement strategies of multi-sectoral coordination during COVID-19, with comparative insight between LMICs and high-income countries (HICs).</jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Eligibility criteria</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>Empirical studies (qualitative, quantitative, or mixed-methods) published in English between 1 January 2020 and 15 August 2024 that examine any coordination mechanism (e.g., task forces, public-private partnerships, inter-agency committees) related to COVID-19 response.</jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Information sources</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>PubMed, EBSCOhost, Emerald Insight, Google Scholar, and targeted grey-literature repositories will be searched; reference lists and citation chaining will supplement database queries.</jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Risk-of-bias assessment</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>Two reviewers will independently appraise qualitative studies with CASP and non-randomised quantitative studies with ROBINS-I; disagreements will be resolved by consensus or third-reviewer adjudication.</jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Data synthesis</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>Owing to the anticipated dominance of qualitative evidence, a CFIR-anchored framework synthesis will be conducted. Quantitative findings will be narratively summarised and, where outcomes are commensurate, explored for fixed-effect pooling. Subgroup analyses will contrast LMIC versus HIC contexts; sensitivity analyses will exclude studies at serious/critical risk of bias. Confidence in cumulative evidence will be graded with CERQual (qualitative) and GRADE (quantitative).</jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Ethics and dissemination</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>No new human data will be collected; therefore additional REC approval is unnecessary. The overarching PhD project holds approvals from the University of KwaZulu-Natal (BREC/00007520/2024) and Kenya’s NACOSTI (NACOSTI/P/24/37716). Results will be disseminated via open-access publication, conference presentation, and policy briefs to Nairobi County health stakeholders.</jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Strengths and Limitations of This Study</jats:title>
                  <jats:list list-type="bullet">
                    <jats:list-item>
                      <jats:p>Comprehensive multi-database and gray-literature searching will minimise retrieval bias</jats:p>
                    </jats:list-item>
                    <jats:list-item>
                      <jats:p>Two reviewers will independently screen, extract and appraise studies, enhancing methodological rigour.</jats:p>
                    </jats:list-item>
                    <jats:list-item>
                      <jats:p>Framework synthesis mapped to the consolidated framework for implementation research (CFIR) domains provides a transparent, theory-driven structure for integrating heterogeneous qualitative evidence.</jats:p>
                    </jats:list-item>
                    <jats:list-item>
                      <jats:p>CASP and ROBINS-I instruments will standardise risk-of-bias assessment across qualitative and non-randomised quantitative studies.</jats:p>
                    </jats:list-item>
                    <jats:list-item>
                      <jats:p>
                        <jats:bold>Limitation:</jats:bold>
                        English-language restriction and the paucity of quantitative studies preclude meta-analysis and may introduce language bias.
                      </jats:p>
                    </jats:list-item>
                  </jats:list>
                </jats:sec>
title: Multi-sectoral Coordination During the COVID-19 Pandemic: Practices, Challenges, and Recommendations for Future Preparedness - A Systematic Literature Review Protocol

reference.unstructured: Taylor P , Mccarthy M. Building a Better World: The Crisis and Opportunity of Covid-19. IDS Bull [Internet]. 2021; Available from: https://consensus.app/papers/building-a-better-world-the-crisis-and-opportunity-of-taylor-mccarthy/ca67f64521f757328efd09e739e0f148/
